topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the blunt finality of a closing door. Dr. Albright was departing. My stomach tightened into a hard knot, a cold, heavy pressure that made breathing shallow. I reread the terse message, the carefully chosen words sounding like a deliberate dismissal. I stared at the screen, my fingers hovering over the keyboard, unable to form a coherent response. Every line of his previous guidance, every late-night session spent poring over my drafts, suddenly felt tainted, recontextualized as something less than valuable. When I finally closed the laptop, the silence of my cramped dorm room was deafening, amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest. I walked to the window, watching the campus lights blur, feeling a profound sense of being overlooked, a sudden, sharp realization that the scaffolding supporting my entire project was being dismantled by someone I had relied upon.

[story 2]

"So, this is it?" I managed, the words scraping out of my throat. The air in the faculty lounge felt thick and stagnant, suffocating. Professor Davies leaned back in his chair, a practiced, easy smile plastered on his face, but his eyes held a flicker of something distant, like he was already halfway out the door. He spoke of new opportunities, of exciting ventures, and the way he phrased his departure—as a necessary evolution—felt like a thinly veiled critique of my slow, methodical pace. I gripped my lukewarm coffee mug so tightly my knuckles turned white. My jaw ached. I didn't look at him; instead, I focused intently on the condensation tracing paths down the side of the mug. The sheer dismissal in his tone made my hands tremble, a barely contained tremor that I fought desperately to suppress.

[story 3]

I stood in the hallway just outside the department office, the polished linoleum reflecting the harsh fluorescent lights above. The news had hit me like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. I watched a colleague approach Dr. Albright, their conversation casual, friendly. Every shared glance between them seemed to confirm the shift in power, the quiet acknowledgment that my specific trajectory, my reliance on his expertise, was now obsolete. A hot flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks crimson. I felt a sudden, intense urge to walk away, to simply vanish from this space, to prove that his departure didn't diminish my capability, even though the thought felt utterly hollow. My feet felt rooted to the spot, paralyzed by a simmering resentment that burned just beneath the surface of my composure.
